activities.title: Daily Chaos Triage
activities.description: A morning production alert is screaming louder than office chai machine. Pick the first safe move.
activities.prompt: What should you do first when a login error suddenly spikes after release?
options: Patch directly in production because speed is king
Check logs, confirm scope, communicate impact, then plan rollback/fix
Ask the intern to restart random servers
Ignore it until the client calls twice
correctAnswers: Check logs, confirm scope, communicate impact, then plan rollback/fix
activities.successMessage: Good triage! The bug is still ugly, but at least it is wearing an ID card now.
activities.failureMessage: The bug escaped wearing sunglasses. Funny, but your first response needs evidence before action.
activities.learningPoint: Incident response starts with verification, impact assessment, communication, and rollback readiness.
activities.practicalTakeaway: Before fixing, identify what changed, who is affected, and what safe rollback path exists.

activities.title: Bug Hunt: Login Safari
activities.description: The app works on everyone’s laptop except the client’s phone, which is apparently haunted.
activities.prompt: Select the strongest evidence items for a useful bug report.
options: Exact device/browser/app version
Repro steps with expected vs actual result
Screenshot or recording
Only write: not working please check
Impact/severity note
correctAnswers: Repro steps with expected vs actual result
activities.successMessage: Evidence captured. The bug can no longer hide behind ‘works on my machine’.
activities.failureMessage: The bug report went to gym but skipped leg day: weak evidence, weak reproduction.
activities.learningPoint: A useful bug report reduces ambiguity and helps developers reproduce quickly.
activities.practicalTakeaway: Always include environment, steps, expected/actual behavior, evidence, and severity context.

activities.title: Data Cleanup Race
activities.description: A spreadsheet has duplicate customers, mystery blanks, and one row named ‘asdfasdf’ with full confidence.
activities.prompt: Which actions are privacy-safe cleanup steps?
options: Identify duplicate rows before deletion
Validate suspicious data against approved source
Send the full sheet to a WhatsApp group for fast help
Keep an audit note of changes
Guess missing phone numbers from memory
correctAnswers: Validate suspicious data against approved source
Keep an audit note of changes
activities.successMessage: Clean sheet, clean conscience. The spreadsheet stopped making ghost noises.
activities.failureMessage: The spreadsheet is cleaner, but privacy is now standing outside with a complaint form.
activities.learningPoint: Data cleanup must protect privacy, preserve audit trail, and avoid guessing.
activities.practicalTakeaway: Validate against approved sources and document changes instead of sharing raw data casually.

activities.title: Client Negotiation: One Small Change™
activities.description: The client says it is a tiny change. The Jira ticket is already sweating.
options: Accept immediately to look helpful
Reject rudely because scope is sacred
Clarify impact, offer options, confirm priority and change approval
Tell the team to work weekend silently
correctAnswers: Clarify impact, offer options, confirm priority and change approval
activities.successMessage: Scope controlled without drama. Somewhere a Gantt chart smiled.
activities.failureMessage: The scope grew legs, arms, and a subscription plan. Negotiate with evidence next time.
activities.learningPoint: Scope control is not saying no; it is making trade-offs visible.
activities.practicalTakeaway: Use impact, options, and approval flow before accepting new work.

activities.title: Ethical Dilemma: Red Flag Rush
activities.description: A patient reports worrying symptoms while the waiting room looks like a railway platform.
activities.prompt: What is the safest learning-game response?
options: Give a diagnosis in the app and move on
Suggest medicine dosage because confidence looks professional
Identify red flags, communicate calmly, document, and escalate to qualified urgent care
Make a joke and ignore the concern
correctAnswers: Identify red flags, communicate calmly, document, and escalate to qualified urgent care
activities.successMessage: Safe escalation chosen. Humor stayed outside the emergency door where it belongs.
activities.failureMessage: Unsafe shortcut detected. In medical content, red flags need escalation, not guesswork.
activities.learningPoint: This game teaches triage thinking and communication, not diagnosis or prescriptions.
activities.practicalTakeaway: For concerning symptoms, escalate to qualified medical/emergency care and document clearly.

activities.title: Role Quiz: Feedback Without Fireworks
activities.description: A candidate needs feedback. The wrong sentence can become a legal boomerang with glitter.
activities.prompt: Which feedback style is safest and most professional?
options: Give role-related, evidence-based feedback with respectful wording
Comment on personality and personal background
Say nothing because silence is easiest
Send a meme instead of feedback
correctAnswers: Give role-related, evidence-based feedback with respectful wording
activities.successMessage: Clean feedback! The candidate learned something and Legal did not faint.
activities.failureMessage: Feedback slipped on a banana peel. Keep it role-related, respectful, and evidence-based.
activities.learningPoint: HR feedback should focus on role criteria and observable evidence.
activities.practicalTakeaway: Avoid personal, biased, or legally risky comments; use structured scorecard evidence.

activities.title: Weekly Boss Battle: Coming Soon
activities.description: A future weekly challenge will combine role constraints, timers, and branching consequences.
options: Coming soon
activities.failureMessage: Weekly challenge is not active yet.
activities.practicalTakeaway: Return later for rotating constraints and rewards.
